q1.prompt: A gymnast performs a slow, balanced pose followed by an explosive leap. According to the size principle of motoneuron recruitment, which of the following describes the activity of her motoneuron pool during this progression?

options.label: Small motoneurons are recruited first to generate precise force, followed by larger motoneurons for the leap.
Large motoneurons are recruited first to overcome inertia, followed by smaller ones for stabilization.
All motoneurons in the pool fire simultaneously to ensure maximal force for the leap.
Recruitment order is determined solely by the velocity of the intended movement, not the size of the neuron.
Gamma-motoneurons are inhibited during the pose to prevent unwanted muscle twitches.

q2.prompt: During a neurological examination, a patellar tendon tap elicits a brisk "knee jerk." Which of the following describes the coordinated synaptic events occurring in the antagonistic muscle (semitendinosus) during this reflex?

options.label: Monosynaptic excitation by Group Ia afferent fibers leading to contraction.
Activation of an inhibitory interneuron in the spinal cord leading to hyperpolarization of the alpha-motoneuron.
Direct inhibition by Group Ib afferent fibers resulting in relaxation.
Activation of gamma-motoneurons to maintain the sensitivity of the opposing spindles.
Suppression of Renshaw cell activity to allow for unopposed limb extension.

q3.prompt: A researcher is recording action potentials from a single sensory fiber during a controlled muscle stretch. The recording shows a high-frequency burst of spikes during the dynamic phase of the stretch, which then settles into a slower, steady rate when the muscle length is held constant. This fiber is most likely a:

options.label: Group Ib fiber from a Golgi tendon organ.
Group II fiber from a nuclear chain fiber.
Group Ia fiber from a nuclear bag fiber.
Group IV fiber from a polymodal nociceptor.
B fiber from a preganglionic autonomic neuron.

q4.prompt: An athlete is performing a maximal isometric bicep curl. As the tension in the tendon reaches a critical level that threatens tissue integrity, the muscle suddenly relaxes. What is the mediating mechanism for this inverse myotatic reflex?

options.label: Activation of muscle spindles which inhibit the homonymous muscle.
Inhibition of the alpha-motoneuron by a direct monosynaptic link from the tendon.
Excitation of the antagonistic muscle via Group Ia afferent fibers.
Group Ib afferents stimulate an inhibitory interneuron that synapses on the homonymous alpha-motoneuron.
Recurrent inhibition by Renshaw cells responding to excessive muscular force.

q5.prompt: If a pharmacological agent were administered that selectively blocked gamma-motoneurons while leaving alpha-motoneurons fully functional, what would be the observed effect on Group Ia afferent activity during a voluntary muscle contraction?

options.label: Ia firing would increase because the spindle would be stretched by the contracting extrafusal fibers.
Ia firing would remain constant due to the inherent stability of the spindle receptor.
Ia firing would cease because the spindle would be crushed by the shortening of the muscle.
Ia firing would be maintained at a steady rate by the compensatory action of the Golgi tendon organ.
Ia firing would decrease or stop because the spindle would go slack as the muscle shortens.

q6.prompt: An experimental toxin is discovered that specifically destroys alpha-motoneurons but completely spares gamma-motoneurons. What is the expected functional status of a target muscle in an animal treated with this toxin?

options.label: The muscle would be unable to generate force, but spindle sensitivity could still be modulated.
The muscle would exhibit spasticity because the gamma-loop would be unopposed.
The muscle would contract normally, but it would lose all sensory proprioceptive feedback.
The muscle would undergo hypertrophy due to constant stimulation of the intrafusal fibers.
The muscle would be paralyzed and the stretch reflex would be completely abolished.

q7.prompt: A patient experiences generalized muscle convulsions after accidental exposure to strychnine. Strychnine causes these symptoms by acting as a competitive antagonist at glycine receptors. Which component of the spinal motor circuit is most directly impaired?

options.label: The Ia afferent synapse onto the alpha-motoneuron.
The Renshaw cell feedback loop that normally provides recurrent inhibition.
The excitatory interneurons involved in the crossed-extension reflex.
The alpha-motoneuron terminal at the neuromuscular junction.
The gamma-motoneuron innervation of the nuclear bag fibers.

q8.prompt: A patient with a chronic upper motor neuron (UMN) lesion exhibits a clasp-knife reflex during passive flexion of the arm. Which of the following best describes the physiological transition that occurs at the moment the resistance suddenly "melts away"?

options.label: Activation of the stretch reflex in the antagonistic muscle group.
Sudden inactivation of the voltage-gated sodium channels in the alpha-motoneuron.
The Golgi tendon reflex overcomes the hyperactive stretch reflex, causing sudden relaxation.
Fatigue of the neuromuscular junction following sustained muscle contraction.
Activation of the primary motor cortex to voluntarily override the spinal reflex.

q9.prompt: During the final miles of a marathon, which motor units are the last to be recruited and are most likely to show signs of significant metabolic fatigue?

options.label: Slow-oxidative (Type I) units, which have a high density of mitochondria.
Fast-oxidative-glycolytic (Type IIa) units, which utilize primarily aerobic pathways.
Units innervated by the smallest motoneurons in the motoneuron pool.
Fast-glycolytic (Type IIb) units, which are innervated by large, high-threshold motoneurons.
Muscle units consisting primarily of intrafusal fibers and gamma-motoneurons.

q10.prompt: A subject is maintaining a steady-state (static) posture. Which sensory ending is providing the central nervous system with a continuous, non-adapting signal regarding the absolute length of the skeletal muscle?

options.label: Primary (annulospiral) endings of Ia fibers on nuclear bag fibers.
Rapidly adapting Pacinian corpuscles within the muscle belly.
Nociceptors on free nerve endings detecting metabolic byproducts.
Golgi tendon organs responding to the steady-state tension of the tendon.
Secondary (flower-spray) endings of Group II afferents on nuclear chain fibers.

q11.prompt: Analyze the functional logic behind the classification of the stretch reflex as monosynaptic and the Golgi tendon reflex as disynaptic.

options.label: The Ia afferent synapses directly on the alpha-motoneuron, while the Ib afferent requires an intervening inhibitory interneuron.
The Ia afferent requires an excitatory interneuron, while the Ib afferent is a bare nerve ending.
The stretch reflex involves both alpha and gamma motoneurons, requiring an extra synapse.
The Golgi tendon reflex is faster because it involves only one synapse in the spinal cord.
The stretch reflex must pass through the dorsal column nuclei before returning to the muscle.

q12.prompt: A patient is in the "spinal shock" phase following a traumatic complete spinal cord transection. What is the underlying physiological reason for the initial areflexia and flaccidity seen below the level of the lesion?

options.label: Loss of the tonic excitatory influence from descending supraspinal pathways on the motoneurons.
Total destruction of the spinal reflex arcs at every segmental level.
Massive release of inhibitory neurotransmitters like GABA and glycine from the brain stem.
Irreversible damage to the primary afferent Group Ia and Group Ib fibers.
Autonomic failure leading to a lack of blood flow to the spinal gray matter.

q13.prompt: A man steps on a sharp tack and his leg remains flexed for several seconds even after the painful stimulus is removed. This persistent motor activity, known as an afterdischarge, is primarily the result of:

options.label: Prolonged binding of glutamate to the nicotinic receptors at the neuromuscular junction.
Continued firing of the nociceptors due to persistent tissue damage.
Recurrent activity within polysynaptic reflex circuits in the spinal cord.
The slow reuptake of calcium by the sarcoplasmic reticulum in the skeletal muscle.
Voluntary effort by the cerebral cortex to keep the foot away from the floor.

q14.prompt: How does the anatomical arrangement of the muscle spindle determine its unique functional role compared to the Golgi tendon organ?

options.label: Spindles are in series with extrafusal fibers to detect tension; GTOs are in parallel to detect length.
Spindles are in parallel to detect tension; GTOs are in series to detect length.
Spindles are found only in the tendons, whereas GTOs are distributed throughout the muscle belly.
Spindles are in parallel to detect changes in length; GTOs are in series to detect changes in tension.
Spindles are only innervated by sensory fibers, whereas GTOs have both sensory and motor supply.

q15.prompt: A subject performs the Jendrassik maneuver (hooking fingers together and pulling) while their patellar reflex is tested. Why does this maneuver result in a more pronounced "knee jerk"?

options.label: It increases the sensitivity of the alpha-motoneuron to all inhibitory inputs.
It causes a direct mechanical stretch of the quadriceps muscle.
It inhibits the Ia afferent fibers, allowing for a more explosive response when they fire.
It activates the Golgi tendon organ, which facilitates the stretch reflex.
It increases gamma-motoneuron discharge, which increases the sensitivity of the muscle spindle.

q16.prompt: An alpha-motoneuron in the spinal cord receives simultaneous excitatory inputs from a Group Ia fiber and inhibitory inputs from a Renshaw cell. If the net potential at the axon hillock is -65 mV and the firing threshold is -60 mV, what is the resulting motor response?

options.label: No action potential is fired, as the summated inputs failed to reach the threshold of the spike initiation zone.
A smaller-than-normal action potential is fired due to the presence of the inhibitory input.
An action potential is fired because excitatory inputs always take precedence over inhibitory ones.
A single twitch is produced in the muscle unit because the cell is in its relative refractory period.
The motoneuron fires at a lower frequency, encoding a weaker muscle contraction.

q17.prompt: A patient with cerebellar disease exhibits hypotonia. Based on the components of the motor system, this decrease in the resistance to passive stretch is best explained by:

options.label: A total loss of alpha-motoneurons in the spinal cord.
A decrease in gamma-motoneuron activity, which reduces the baseline sensitivity of the muscle spindles.
Overactivity of the Golgi tendon reflex, which causes constant muscle relaxation.
Destruction of the extrafusal muscle fibers by circulating antibodies.
Blockade of the voltage-gated calcium channels in the T-tubules of the skeletal muscle.

q18.prompt: A peripheral nerve is subjected to significant localized external pressure, leading to temporary ischemia. Which of the following sensory modalities will be lost first?

options.label: Dull, aching pain and temperature sensation.
Perception of pleasant aromas and flavors.
Conscious proprioception and fine, discriminative touch.
Deep visceral pain from the abdominal organs.
Autonomic control of the heart rate and blood pressure.

q19.prompt: During the crossed-extension reflex, what specific synaptic event occurs in the contralateral flexor muscle to ensure it remains relaxed?

options.label: Monosynaptic excitation by the nociceptor afferent fiber.
Direct inhibition of the muscle fiber by the release of glycine from the motor nerve.
Activation of gamma-motoneurons to stiffen the contralateral spindle.
Activation of an inhibitory interneuron leading to hyperpolarization of the alpha-motoneuron.
Suppression of the Golgi tendon organ to prevent any unwanted relaxation.

q20.prompt: A single muscle twitch produces a force of 10g, but tetanic stimulation (high frequency) produces a force of 50g. What is the primary physiological basis for this five-fold increase in tension?

options.label: Recruitment of additional, larger motor units from the motoneuron pool.
Increased amplitude of each individual muscle action potential.
The activation of more nicotinic receptors at the motor end plate.
An increase in the number of alpha-motoneurons firing action potentials.
Sustained high intracellular calcium levels that maintain cross-bridge cycling and fully stretch the series elastic component.
